coredata.dc:title: Paper strip based and live cell ultrasensitive lead sensor using carbon dots synthesized from biological media
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Abhishek
Verma, Navneet Chandra
Khan, Syamantak
Tiwari, Shalini
Chaudhary, Abhishek
Nandi, Chayan Kanti
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots), a new class of non-toxic carbogenic nanomaterials have attracted lots of attention. By using simple microwave heating of a biocompatible biological media such as potato-dextrose agar (PDA), the newly synthesized C-dots not only showed its potential application on the ultrasensitive and highly selective Lead (Pb2+) detection in solution but also in paper based sensor strips. The detection limit (DL) of Pb2+ was found to be 106–110pM both in solution and on a paper based strips sensor. The fast kinetic data suggested on spot real time Pb2+ detection. Finally, it showed the potential Pb2+ sensing in human cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biological media
Potato-dextrose agar
Sensor strip
Detection limit
Human cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
Preparation of paper-based strips of C-dots and metal- ion sensing test
Imaging of UV–vis color changes in solution phase
Confocal imaging of live cells (Human cell lines)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the synthesized C-dots (inset showing the particle distribution) and (b) AFM images and the height profile of the synthesized C-dots.
(a) XPS scanning spectrum shows three major peaks of C1s, N1s, and O1s, with C:O:N ratio as 58.64: 34.89: 6.47. (b), (c) and (d) shows the high resolution C1s, N1s and O1s XPS spectra.

(a) FTIR spectra before and after binding with Pb2+ (inset: change in zeta potential) (b) Fluorescence quenching of C-dots upon addition of Pb2+ (inset: UV lamp images of the same) (c) Titrimetric quenching fluorescence of C-dots with increasing concentration of Pb2+ (inset: Stern Volmer plot for calculating DL) and (d) the kinetics data of fluorescence quenching upon addition of Pb2+.

(a) Images of C-dots tethered paper-based sensor strips upon addition of different concentration of Pb2+. (b) Stern Volmer fitting of the image (a) for DL calculation. (c) Images of C-dots tethered paper-based sensor strips upon addition of different metal ions (50μМ). (d) & (e) photograph using a 365nm UV lamp of only C-dots (left), after addition of Rhodamine B or Cyanine 5 (middle) and followed by addition of 50μM Pb2+ in the solution (right).

Multicolor confocal images of MDA MB 231 cells at different conditions under 401, 532 and 635nm laser (a) stained with only C-dots, (b) C-dots stained MDA MB 231 after Pb2+ treatment, resulted all color diminished in all channel (c) multicolor imaging of MDA MB 231 treatment with C-dots −RhB and (d) C-dots-RhB stained MDA MB cells incubated with Pb2+ (complete quenching in blue region), color of RhB (green and red) exist in the cells. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Paper strip based and live cell ultrasensitive lead sensor using carbon dots synthesized from biological media

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi, Himachal Pradesh, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology, Mandi, Himachal Pradesh 175001, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots), a new class of non-toxic carbogenic nanomaterials have attracted lots of attention. By using simple microwave heating of a biocompatible biological media such as potato-dextrose agar (PDA), the newly synthesized C-dots not only showed its potential application on the ultrasensitive and highly selective Lead (Pb2+) detection in solution but also in paper based sensor strips. The detection limit (DL) of Pb2+ was found to be 106–110pM both in solution and on a paper based strips sensor. The fast kinetic data suggested on spot real time Pb2+ detection. Finally, it showed the potential Pb2+ sensing in human cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Accumulation of any forms of lead (Pb2+) in human body is highly toxic. It alters the development of brain, memory function, reproductive system and cardiovascular function, causing serious health risk especially in children [1–3]. Because of such toxic and carcinogenic effect Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the limit of Pb2+ intake in drinking water less than 72nM (15.0ppb) [4]. Over the past decades, several standard techniques have been reported for the ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+ (Table S1 and references therein) [5–10]. However, all these techniques have several disadvantages like require costly instruments, have complexity in sample preparation, time consuming and moreover require trained personal [11]. As a result they are not very much useful for on-site detection. Fluorescence based method using organic probes, especially “turn-on” fluorescence mechanism, although because of its high sensitivity is highly desirable, but are rarely reported [12–14]. In recent years, functionalized gold nanoparticles have been designed for highly sensitive and selective Pb2+ detection (Table S1 and references therein) [15,16]. However, expensive reagents, accurate control, harsh acidic or basic medium and moreover tedious multistep time consuming processes complicate the detection.

In this report, we have demonstrated a simple but elegant approach for the ultrasensitive and highly selective Pb2+ detection by using C-dots, which are a new class of carbogenic nanomaterials. C-dots have attracted tremendous interest in recent years, because of the several advantages such as high solubility, resistance to photo bleaching, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility [17–19]. Recently, highly singlet oxygen generation approach has been developed for photodynamic therapy via using graphene quantum dots [20]. Moreover, the red emissive carbon dots found to be the suitable candidate for fluorescence and photo acoustic bioimaging [21]. However, the origin of multicolor fluorescence of carbon dots is not clear till date and remains a topic of debate. The recent studies show that the presence of different surface defects and energy traps are the most probable origin of fluorescence [22]. Whether the fluorescence originates from a single particle with multichromophoric groups or from different particles, is still debatable [23].

Furthermore, unlike the semiconductor quantum dots or metal nanoparticles, C-dots could easily be synthesized using a single step reaction. For example, recently nitrogen and sulfur doped C-dots were synthesized by our group, for the ultrasensitive mercury (Hg2+) and naked eye colorimetric arsenic [As(III)] detection [24,25]. Although, few reports are available on Pb2+ detection using C-dots however, they suffer either from poor sensitivity or selectivity [26–29].

A novel single step method has been established to synthesize bright, fluorescent C-dots using microwave assisted pyrolysis. We have used a simple microwave heating of biological media (PDA) just for 3min for the synthesis of C-dots and at a later stage a rather simple purification process. Atomic force microscopy (AFM), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and various other spectroscopic analysis such as UV–vis absorbance, Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed to characterize the synthesized C-dots. The prepared fluorescent C-dots are water soluble and showed strong multicolor fluorescence. The newly synthesized C-dots showed ultrasensitive and highly selective Pb2+ detection not only in solution but also in paper based sensor strips.

All Glass wares were washed with aqua regia (3HCl: 1HNO3), followed by rinsing several times with double distilled water. Potato dextrose agar (PDA), NaCl, Rhodamine B dye were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. Cyanine 5 dye purchased from Lumiprobe Corporation. All metal ions including chloride of lead, mercury, chromium, cadmium, magnesium, copper, cobalt, nickel, manganese, zinc and arsenic oxide with purity >99% were purchased from Merck India chemicals. Double distilled deionized water (DI) of 18.3mΩ (Elga Purelab Ultra) was used throughout the preparation of solutions. Cellulose filter membrane (paper strips) with 200nm pore size was purchased from GE Healthcare.

The UV–vis spectroscopy of C-dots and the assay with metal ions were measured using Shimadzu (UV–vis 2450) spectrophotometer. The spectra were collected using 10mm path length quartz cuvette. 1mL of C-dots solution was used to measure the spectra. All the data were repeated at least three times.

The particle size and dispersity of the synthesized nanoparticles were performed using a TECNAI G2 200kV TEM (FEI, Electron Optics) electron microscope with 200kV input voltage. TEM grids were prepared by placing 5μL diluted and well sonicated sample solution on a carbon coated copper grid. The sample solution was eaporated at room temperature. Precautions were taken to avoid contamination from various sources like dust particles.

FTIR spectra of C-Dots were measured using a Perkin-Elmer FTIR spectrophotometer equipped with a horizontal attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessories containing a zinc selenide crystal and operating at 4cm−1 resolution. The use of the spectral subtraction provided reliable and reproducible results. KBr pellets has been used as a reference and for the baseline correction.

Zeta potential measurements were carried out on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano system using with 633nm He-Ne laser. 1mL of the sample was used in a cuvette and measured for 10 runs in 10s time interval. The average of atleast five measurements were reported in each data.

To determine the particle size AFM analysis of the synthesized C-dots was carried out using a Digital Instruments Bruker AFM. Standard Veeco tapping mode silicon probes were used for scanning the samples. Typically, aqueous suspensions of C-dots samples were dried on silicon substrate for 3h. Once dried, samples were placed on the AFM and scanned.

X-ray Photo-Electron Spectroscopy (XPS) with Auger Electron Spectroscopy (AES) module PHI 5000 Versa Prob II, FEI Inc. and C60 sputter gun has been used for characterization and scanning the spectra from C1s to O1s region. Al Κα X-ray radiation was used as the source for excitation (1486.8eV, 500mm). Samples were loaded on copper strips and surface adherence done by double sided adhesive tape. High resolution scan was also performed for C1s, N1s and O1s to get the information for functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Steady state fluorescence was measured using Horiba Fluorolog-3 Spectrofluorometer. All the experiments including Pb2+detection were performed at room temperature after equilibrating the samples for 15min. The fluorescence was measured in 1mL quartz cuvette. For titration experiment, to calculate the detection limit, different concentration of Pb2+ ions were added to a fixed concentration of C-dots and the final volume is maintained as 1mL. For determining the selectivity for Pb2+ ion, 50μM of each metal ion were added into a fixed concentration of C-dots. The kinetics of the detection was investigated by recording the fluorescence spectra of C-dots, immediately after the addition of Pb2+ ion up to 5min (as most of the quenching processes were over within 5min of the contact time). The fluorescence lifetime was measured using Horiba scientific Delta Flex TCSPC system with Pulsed LED Sources. Ludox has been used as an IRF for deconvolution of the spectral value. 3mL C-dots solution has been used for all the lifetime measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based strips of C-dots and metal- ion sensing test
ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose-based filter paper (OE 66) was used (diameter of 25mm) and immobilized the C-dots solution on it. After 5min, the filter paper was removed from the solution and air-dried. The paper-based strip containing C-dots was exposed to metal ions in aqueous solution. After 5min, the paper-based strip was removed from the aqueous solution of metal ions and air-dried at room temperature. The changes in the fluorescence of the strip were investigated with a UV–vis illuminator (365nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of UV–vis color changes in solution phase
ce:section.ce:para: UV vis color changes Imaging has been taken by using NIKON L820 with 30× optical zoom. 3mL of C-dots, C-dots- RhB/Cy5 and C-dots-Pb2+–RhB/Cy5 solution combination took in a quartz cuvette and illuminated in 365nm UV–vis lamp source.

A human cancer cell line MDA MB 231 and HeLa cells were obtained from NCCS Pune. All cell culture related reagents were purchased from sigma. Cells were grown in normal DMEM culture medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. During staining of cell lines, Lab-Tek® Chamber slide used, where cell lines medium (with diffused cells) solution mixed with the same amount of synthesized C-dots as well as Rhodamine B (1μМ) solution in CO2 incubator (Temp. 25°C and 5% CO2) for 24h incubation. Adhesive cell containing slide has been used after three washing with 1X PBS solution for removal of extra C-dots and cell culture medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal imaging of live cells (Human cell lines)
ce:section.ce:para: Imaging of HeLa cells, MDA MB 231 cells was performed using Nikon Eclipse TI-U system, equipped with three lasers 401, 488 and 639nm wavelengths and corresponding filters. The images were analyzed using software NIS Element AR. 60x Nikon oil immersion objective having 1.4 NA was used. For confocal Imaging the cells were incubated with C-dots 1 for 2h.

For the synthesis of C-dots, 2g of PDA broth was dissolved in 2mL of distilled water for microwave heating. The mixture was heated at 600W for 3min using a normal microwave. The resulting material (chocolate brown ash) is diluted up to 100mL in distilled water and the solution was filtered by Whatman filter paper. The solution was ultracentrifuge (sorvall Lynx 6000, thermo scientific) for two times successively at 23000rpm. Each run was carried out for 30min. The pellets were removed each time and the final supernatant was collected.

An usual multicolor fluorescence [30,31] was observed with the emission maximum at 415nm, when excited at 310nm (Fig. S1). A comparison in quantum yield (∼9.0%) of the synthesized C-dots with the reported values suggested that, it could efficiently been used potentially for sensors and bioimaging (Table S2). Although the origin of such multicolor fluorescence is still debatable because of its unknown structure, recently we have shown that the inhomogeneous broadening and spectral migration suggested as one of the key origin [32]. The time resolved emission spectroscopic data suggested that the commonly observed red-shifting phenomena originates from an individual entity with an ensemble of energy sub-states, when solvent relaxation and fluorescence lifetime matches with each other (Fig. S1). The chemical environment, such as pH, temperature and concentration also played important role on the fluorescence of the C-dots. An electron transfer process by the creation of cationic dark state has also been proposed [33].

As Pb2+ is very much prone to form complex with both the OH and COOH functional groups [35–38], we inspired to check whether the C-dots could be used for the potential Pb2+ sensor. Extensive quenching of the C-dots fluorescence at 415nm (excited at 310nm) was observed upon addition of 50μM of Pb2+. This data suggested that the present C-dots forms complex with Pb2+ and can sense it efficiently (Fig. 3b). The inset showed the UV lamp color changes on addition of Pb2+. A decrease in excited state lifetime confirms that the quenching process most probably takes place via an excited state electron transfer reaction (Fig. S2).

The formation of C-dots and Pb2+ complex was further confirmed by the decrease in zeta potential from −21.5 to −14.7mV (inset of Fig. 3a). An extensive decrease in intensity for the peaks at 1308 and 724cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3a) confirms the probable binding of Pb2+ to the COOH and OH functional groups of the C-dots. To determine the efficiency of sensing, the DL was calculated by performing a titration experiment on the quenching of C-dots fluorescence with increase in concentration of Pb2+ (between 0 and 2×104 nM) (Fig. 3c). The DL was estimated as low as 110pM at a signal to noise ratio of 3 with a fitting parameter of 0.996 (inset of Fig. 3c). This is the so far highest ultrasensitive Pb2+ sensing using any C-dots material and is much lower than the EPA approved limit [4]. Interestingly, the C-dots also have capability to detect Pb2+ with a DL of 270 and 132pM at 5 and 9pH respectively. The data suggests that the C-dots could efficiently been used for Pb2+ detection under various pH (Fig. S3). The kinetic experiment showed that Pb2+ could be detected within ∼3min after the incubation of C-dots with it (Fig. 3d).

The selectivity, which is one of the most important criterion for a good sensor, was checked in presence of various metal ions (50μM). Apart from a little quenching with Cr3+, the sensor was highly selective for Pb2+ detection (Fig. S4a). Further, the fluorescence of the C-dots was measured in presence of all metal ions including and excluding Pb2+ (50μM) to check whether the assay could detect Pb2+ ion efficiently in presence of other metal ions. Fig. S4b showed that there was no change in the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in presence of all the metal ions when Pb2+ was absent however, extensive quenching was observed upon addition of Pb2+ to the system. The excellent specificity combined with high sensitivity and fast response of C-dots to Pb2+ suggested that our method might directly be applied for detecting Pb2+ in real samples. The efficiency of C-dots for detecting Pb2+ in real river water (obtained from the Uhl river in Mandi district, Himachal Pradesh province, India; the physicochemical composition of the river and tap water are given in Table S3 & S4) and in tap water was explored. The water samples, at first, were filtered through a 0.22μm membrane, centrifuged at 13,000rpm for 20min [24,39] and then the supernatant was spiked with standard solutions containing different concentrations of Pb2+. Interestingly, the DL for both the water samples was found to be 1.1 and 0.73nM respectively (Fig. S5), these data suggest that in spite of the presence of several interference from numerous minerals and organics existing in river and tap water, the present C-dots sensor could sense Pb2+ very efficiently.

Encouraged by the solution data, the sensing efficiency was performed on a paper based sensor strip. Cellulosic filter papers, which are of low cost and easy to handle have been used for the study. The C-dots tethered paper based strip was exposed to an aqueous solution of Pb2+ for 5min and then dried before taking the images using a gel doc illuminator equipped with a 365nm UV lamp. Fig. 4 a shows that similar to fluorescence quenching experiment in solution, the intensity of images of the paper strips decreased with increasing concentration of Pb2+. With a good linear correlation upto 1μM, The DL (Fig. 4b) for the paper based strip was obtained as 106 pM (S/N=3), which is similar to the solution based quenching [40]. This indicated that the paper based sensor was as good as solution based sensor, even with a larger dynamic range. To check the selectivity, C-dots containing paper-based strip was exposed to various other metal ions.

Except Pb2+, no color change of the C-dots tethered strip was observed in presence of other metal ions, suggesting the high selectivity (Fig. 4c). To obtain a better contrast on the UV lamp based color change, two fluorescent dyes (RhB and Cy5) of different color were individually added to the C-dots solution. As shown in Fig. 4d and e, the blue color of the C-dots solution converted to light grey and magenta upon mixing with RhB or Cy5 respectively. Interestingly, upon addition of Pb2+ in both the cases original color of the dyes appeared, which suggests the quenching of C-dots fluorescence occurred by the addition of Pb2+. The measured confocal images (equipped with 401, 532 and 635nm lasers) of dried spots of the same sample of Fig. 4d and e shows that the dominating blue color of the C-dots has completely vanished and the added either RhB or Cy5 color is present upon addition of Pb2+ (Fig. S6).

The in-vitro MTT assay suggested that the present C-dots are non-toxic with a micromolar dose but less than 10% toxic with a concentration of 15mg/mL (Fig. S7). Although the turn on fluorescence sensors are good for live cell bioimaging, however, using such less toxic and intense fluorescent C-dots, turn off also could effectively been used [41].

The live cell Pb2+ detection was performed using MDA-MB 231 cell lines, which were incubated with C-dots in presence and absence of Pb2+. The multicolor confocal image of the cells after incubation with the C-dots (in absence of Pb2+) has been shown in Fig. 5 a. Similar to the multicolor fluorescence spectrum in solution, the intensity of the images decrease from blue (top right) to green (bottom left) to red (bottom right). The bright field image shows the presence of the cells (top left). A complete quenching of all the color images occurred when Pb2+ was added to the cells (Fig. 5b). A very small change in the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential (Fig. S8), suggests that the quenching of fluorescence is due to the addition of Pb2+ but not due to the possible aggregation that might happen at lower pH in endosome or lysosome. For a better contrast, the same experiment was performed in presence of RhB dye. Now, along with the blue image that is majorly due to the C-dots fluorescence, extensive green and red emissions, because of the added RhB dye were also observed (Fig. 5c). On the other hand, upon addition of Pb2+ (Fig. 5d) although the highly intense blue image of C-dots is completely quenched, but the green and red images in the cells are still present, suggesting the quenching of C-dots fluorescence in presence of Pb2+ ions.

In conclusion, the newly synthesized C-dots from a biological media (PDA) using simple microwave heating was efficient for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of Pb2+ with a DL as low as 110 pM in solution and 106 pM in a paper based sensor strips. The fast kinetic data suggested on spot real time Pb2+ detection. The very less-toxic and biocompatible C-dots were further applied for live cell Pb2+ detection. We hope that the assay will be extremely beneficial for development of low cost and sensitive sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the home institute (IIT Mandi) and Department of Biotechnology (BT/PR4067/BRB/10/1128/2012) for running the experiments. The support of Advanced Materials Research Center (AMRC), IIT Mandi is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.03.110.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A clinical study of the effects of lead poisoning on the intelligence and neurobehavioral abilities of children
Intellectual impairment and blood lead levels
Lead exposure and its impact on immune system: a review

Taking part of the lab to the sample: on-site electrodeposition of pb followed by measurement in a lab using electrothermal, near-torch vaporization sample introduction and inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry
The measurement of Pb isotope ratios in sub-Ng quantities by fast scanning single collector sector field-ICP-MS

Simultaneous determination of arsenic, cadmium, copper, chromium, nickel, lead and thallium in total digested sediment samples and available fractions by electrothermal atomization atomic absorption spectroscopy (ET AAS)
Catalytic and molecular beacons for amplified detection of metal ions and organic molecules with high sensitivity
A highly sensitive and selective biosensing strategy for the detection of Pb2+ ions based on Gr-5 DNAzyme functionalized AuNPs
Electrochemical detection of parts-per-billion lead via an electrode-bound DNAzyme assembly
Label-free DNAzyme-based fluorescing molecular switch for sensitive and selective detection of lead ions
A highly selective fluorescent chemosensor for Pb2+
Highly sensitive and selective turn-on fluorescent chemosensor for Pb2+ and Hg2+ based on a rhodamine-phenylurea conjugate
Dual on-off and off-on switchable oligoaziridine biosensor
Bioinspired target-specific crystallization on peptide nanotubes for ultrasensitive Pb ion detection
Direct visualization of lead corona and its nanomolar colorimetric detection using anisotropic gold nanoparticles
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Carbon quantum dots for optical bioimaging
Carbon nanotubes as photoacoustic molecular imaging agents in living mice
A graphene quantum dot photodynamic therapy agent with high singlet oxygen generation
Red-emissive carbon dots for fluorescent photoacoustic, and thermal theranostics in living mice
Bandgap like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Single-particle fluorescence intensity fluctuations of carbon nanodots
Nitrogen doped thiol functionalized carbon dots for ultrasensitive Hg(II) detection
Carbon dots for naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive arsenic and glutathione detection
A fluorescent turn-off/on method based on carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the sensitive determination of Pb2+ and pyrophosphate in an aqueous solution
N-doped carbon dots synthesized by rapid microwave irradiation as highly fluorescent probes for Pb2+ detection
Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots via simple acid hydrolysis of bovine serum albumin and its potential as sensitive sensing probe for lead(II) ions
Carbon dots rooted agarose hydrogel hybrid platform for optical detection and separation of heavy metal ions
Photoluminescent carbon dots directly derived from polyethylene glycol and their application for cellular imaging
Facile synthesis of oxygen and sulfur co-doped graphitic carbon nitride fluorescent quantum dots and their application for mercury(II) detection and bioimaging
Time resolved emission reveals ensemble of emissive states as the origin of multicolor fluorescence in carbon dots
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
Triazole-acetate functionalized gold nanoparticles for colorimetric Pb(II) sensing
Pb(II) Binding to humic substances: an equilibrium and spectroscopic study
Synthesis, crystal structures, and luminescence properties of three novel lead carboxy-phosphonates with 3D framework structures using rigid aromatic carboxylic acids as second organic ligands
Facile preparation of lignosulfonate – graphene oxide – polyaniline ternary nanocomposite as an effective adsorbent for Pb(II) ions
Fluorescent nanohybrid of gold nanoclusters and quantum dots for visual determination of lead ions
New fluorescent metal-ion detection using a paper-based sensor strip containing tethered rhodamine carbon nanodots
Carbon nanoparticle-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting mercury ions in aqueous media and living cells

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mr. Abhishek Gupta is currently a Ph.D. Scholar at School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi HP India. He obtained his B.Sc. from MJP R University Bareilly India and M.Sc. Devi Ahilya University, Indore India. His recent research is focused on carbon nanoparticles (C-dots) for toxic metal and biomolecule sensing system.

Mr. Navneet C. Verma is currently a Ph.D. Scholar at School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi HP India. He obtained his M.Sc. from Deen Dayal Upadhyay, Gorakhpur University, Gorakhpur India and M.Tech. from Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India.H is recent research is focused on single molecules spectroscopy and super resolution microscopy.

Syamantak khan is a Ph.D. Scholar at School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi HP. He received his Bachelor and Master degree from Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur in 2012. His research interest includes Protein-Nanoparticles interaction, applied nanobiotechnology and single molecule imaging.

Ms. Shalini Tiwari is currently a master student at School of Biotechnology, Devi Ahilya University, Indore, India. He obtained his B.Sc. in Biotechnology. His recent research is focused on the nontoxic, biocompatible and easily uptake metal nanoparticles for bio applications.

Mr. Abhishek Chaudhary is currently a Ph.D. Scholar at School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi HP India. He obtained his B.Sc. from MJP R University Bareilly India, M.Sc. Allahabad University, Allahabad India and M. Tech Anna University, Chennai India. H is recent research is focused on the anisotropic gold nanoparticles for sensors, protein conformation and sustained drug release.

Dr. Chayan Kanti Nandi received his Ph.D. in physical chemistry and laser spectroscopy from the Indian Institute of Technology Kanpur India in 2006. After completing two post-doctoral research works from Goethe University Frankfurt am Main Germany (2006–2009) and Princeton University USA, in 2010 he joined as an assistant Professor in the school of basic sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi, Himachal Pradesh, India. He is the recipient of the prestigious Alexander von Humboldt fellow in 2007. Currently, he is an associate professor with the research interest in the field of material science, especially the use of in nanomaterials for target specific drug delivery, biomolecular nanosensors and protein conformational dynamics.
